chunk_summary: Outcome-based follow-up in pediatric celiac disease focuses on growth, pubertal progression, and bone health. Guidelines recommend early follow-up, serology checks, and stepwise evaluation of non-response to avoid misdiagnosis of refractory disease.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of outcome-based follow-up in pediatric celiac disease management.
Learn the recommended follow-up schedule and serology testing intervals for celiac disease in India.
Identify and evaluate reversible causes of non-response to a gluten-free diet (GFD).
Recognize when to escalate investigations, including endocrinology consultation or repeat endoscopy.
key_facts: ICMR guidelines recommend follow-up at 1–3 months, serology at 6 months, and annual reviews thereafter.
Key pediatric outcomes include catch-up growth, pubertal progression, and bone health, not just stool normalization.
Non-response requires reassessment of gluten exposure, secondary lactose intolerance, parasites, and SIBO in endemic or high-risk settings.
Significant catch-up in height should occur within 1 year of starting a strict GFD; otherwise, further investigation is needed.
Repeat endoscopy is reserved for cases where clinical and nutritional recovery fails despite adherence and addressing reversible causes.
reference_quote: The ICMR guideline recommends early follow-up after 1–3 months (depending on presentation), repeat serology after 6 months, and then yearly review; each visit should assess symptoms, diet adherence, serology, and growth.
document_key_topics: Outcome-based follow-up in pediatric celiac disease
ICMR guidelines for celiac disease management in India
